## Highlights
The Netherlands ran over Sweden 5-1 in Houston and took top spot in Group F. Brian Brobbey led the way with two goals in the first half (5th and 17th minutes) and Cody Gakpo added two more early in the second half (47th and 54th); Crysencio Summerville, who had come on for Donyell Malen, capped the rout in the 89th. After the 2-2 draw with Japan, the Oranje showed all their firepower. Sweden, who had thrashed Tunisia 5-1 in their opener, got a taste of their own medicine and exposed a fragile defense.

Brian Brobbey and Cody Gakpo were the names of the night, each with two goals, in a Dutch attack that clicked in full from start to finish. The Netherlands dominated possession, created chance after chance and were merciless — the mark of a contending side that seems to have found its rhythm after a stuttering opener. In the end, there was even room for Memphis Depay and Noa Lang to earn minutes.
## Sweden's collapse
Sweden have firepower, but defensive fragility cost them dearly: after scoring five against Tunisia, they conceded five to the Netherlands. The team pulled one back through Anthony Elanga in the 59th minute, right after coming on, but never threatened a comeback against Dutch superiority.
## Discipline
A clean match on the Dutch side: all three yellows went to Sweden — Gabriel Gudmundsson (53'), Yasin Ayari (75') and Lucas Bergvall (80') — a reflection of the pressure Sweden were under.
## Group situation
With the rout, the Netherlands took top spot in Group F, level on points and goal difference with Japan. Sweden dropped to 3rd and now face a decisive clash against Japan in the final round.
## Verdict
Message sent by the Netherlands, who won with authority — braces from Brobbey and Gakpo — and established themselves as group favorites. For Sweden, the alert remains: the attack excites, but the defense needs to improve a lot, or they could bid an early farewell to the World Cup.

Finn Surman opened the scoring for the All Whites just 15 minutes in, silencing the Egyptian crowd. The punishment came in three acts in the second half: Mostafa Zico equalized in the 58th, Mohamed Salah turned it around in the 67th and Trezeguet closed the account in the 82nd.
## The Pharaohs' comeback
The first half was a scare for Egypt, who saw Surman capitalize on a set piece and score early. But the interval reorganized the Pharaohs. In the 58th, Mostafa Zico equalized and restored the team's confidence. Nine minutes later, captain Mohamed Salah did what you expect of a star at the World Cup and completed the comeback. To seal the night, Trezeguet — who had come on in the 76th — netted the third in the 82nd, turning the win into a comfortable rout.
## Discipline
A fierce match, but with no sendings-off. Egypt were booked early through Mohanad Lashin, in the 17th. New Zealand were punished more: Sarpreet Singh saw the card in the 20th and Callum McCowatt in the 34th, both while trying to hold back the Egyptian pressure that would grow after the break.
## Group situation
With the comeback, Egypt take the lead of Group G with 4 points in two matches (a win and the 1-1 draw with Belgium), a positive difference of 2. New Zealand, who had drawn 2-2 with Iran in their opener, stall on 1 point and are left with a -2 difference, complicating their qualification math. Belgium and Iran, both on 1 point with a game in hand, remain in the fight.
## Verdict
A win of character for Egypt, who showed maturity to overturn an adverse scoreline away from home and leaned on Salah's talent at the decisive moment. New Zealand pay dearly for not sustaining the tempo in the second half and now need points in the final round to stay alive.
